_comment: tiles2 — breaking change from tiles v1. Each ground type is a SPECIFICALLY named subfolder (saturated_grass, not 'grass') so more variants can be added later. Flow: generate -> raw/ (+ per-sheet metadata, kind=base|transition) -> postprocess copies into base/ or transitions/<other>/, normalizing brightness/hue/saturation to the ref-sprite(s). Raw is always kept so postprocess can be re-tuned and re-run.

prompt._comment: Quality-focused prompts. NO lineless/no-outline (create-tiles-pro bakes an outline in regardless). We deliberately generate MANY base sheets per type, EACH with a different creative `angle` (sheet idx cycles the angle list) to explore the space — earthy sides on one, rocky on the next, flowery, dry, etc. A type may override `base_angles`; otherwise the generic list is used. `variations` gives per-tile micro-variety WITHIN a sheet.
prompt.style: cozy hand-crafted top-down RPG pixel art, rich natural texture, cohesive harmonious palette, soft directional shading, high quality game terrain tile
prompt.base_template: isometric {description} terrain tiles, {angle}, every tile is {name}: {variations}. {style}
prompt.transition_template: isometric terrain tiles blending from {from_desc} to {to_desc}, {angle}: {variations}. {style}
prompt.base_variations: 1) plain even ground 2) soft surface texture 3) a few natural details 4) a slightly darker weathered patch 5) a slightly lighter sunlit patch 6) small scattered specks
base_angles: fine scattered detail and small flecks
a touch darker and cooler in tone
prompt.transition_variations: 1) mostly {from_name} with {to_name} entering from one corner 2) an even diagonal split from {from_name} to {to_name} 3) mostly {to_name} with a patch of {from_name} 4) a soft natural border between {from_name} and {to_name} 5) {to_name} scattered across {from_name} 6) a gradual blend from {from_name} into {to_name}
prompt._transition_angles_note: Each of the 5 sheets per pair uses a different BORDER STYLE (cycled by sheet index), so together they cover every kind of edge a map builder might need.
transition_angles: with a soft ragged natural border
with a patchy scattered border of small islands
with interlocking fingers along the border

ground_types.name: saturated grass
ground_types._angles_note: Keep it PLAIN saturated grass — angles are SMALL variations only (soil sides, slight tone/density, a few tiny accents). No dramatic re-themes (no tall overgrowth, no cliff sides, no dry/bare fields) so the whole type keeps one look & feel.
base_angles: each tile has natural dark soil sides
a slightly denser, richer grass texture
a few tiny scattered flower specks
a couple of small bare earth spots
faint moss and a few tiny pebbles
soil sides with a few small embedded stones
a touch darker and cooler in tone

ground_types.name: dirt
ground_types.description: packed brown dirt with mixed light and dark patches

ground_types.name: grey mountain stone
ground_types.description: rugged grey mountain rock and stone

ground_types.name: wooden balcony
ground_types.description: warm wooden plank balcony floor / deck boards

elevation._comment: base_x_N = themed TALL blocks that stack PIXEL-PERFECTLY on base_x_1: identical 30px diamond top (tile_size 64, view_angle 28, flat_top 2) so the walkable top surface lands in the same place; only the face grows to N*16px (calibrated depth_ratio/tile_height per type — see docs/ELEVATION.md). 5 sheets per type, one per DECORATION. These get NO transitions and are NOT colour-harmonised (multi-coloured props). One 'level' = base_x_1's 16px face.
elevation.style: cozy hand-crafted top-down RPG pixel art, rich natural texture, cohesive harmonious palette, soft directional shading, high quality game asset, transparent background
elevation.template: isometric pixel-art game tile: {decoration}, drawn as {height_word} that fills the tile's full height, with a flat diamond top surface and solid vertical sides, sitting on the ground. {style}. {variations}
elevation.variations: The sheet holds slight natural variations of the same subject — some a touch fuller, some sparser, some lighter, some a little darker, a couple gently turned — but every tile is clearly the same {name}.

types.name: 2-level block
decorations: a mossy grey granite boulder with a little grass on top
a small round leafy bush-tree on a grassy mound
a cluster of glowing pale-blue crystals growing from rock
a cluster of red-capped white-spotted magic mushrooms on a grassy mound
a grassy earth step with exposed soil and small embedded stones on its sides

types.name: 3-level block
decorations: a big leafy green tree with a thick sturdy trunk
a tall glowing pale-blue crystal spire
an ancient weathered carved standing-stone menhir
a balanced stack of mossy grey boulders forming a cairn
a giant red-capped white-spotted mushroom with a thick pale stalk

types.name: 4-level block
decorations: a huge ancient oak tree with a broad leafy canopy and gnarled trunk
a grey stone fortress watchtower with battlements and a small window
a giant faceted glowing pale-blue crystal spire
a steep grassy clifftop with layered exposed rock and soil sides
a jagged rocky mountain spire with a dusting of snow on top

_transitions_comment: FULL MESH — no adjacency list. Map builders must have every border, so each type gets a transition to EVERY other type. The loop completes a type's bases, then generates its transition to every EARLIER type (the new type owns transitions toward all existing ones); a pair is skipped if already covered either direction. Stored under <type>/transitions/<earlier-type>.

targets._comment: base_sheets_per_type raw BASE sheets first, THEN this type's transitions to every earlier type, before the next type. Each sheet = one create-tiles-pro item (~16 tiles). Sync (git<-PixelLab) drops sheets you delete in the UI, so the loop tops back up to target.

budget._comment: Proceed if the subscription pool is above min_generations_remaining OR there are >= min_usd credits (PixelLab bills USD credits once subscription generations hit 0; tiles are cheap).

postprocess._comment: raw -> base/transitions. neutralize_outline softens the outer silhouette rim. harmonize pulls each tile's dominant MATERIAL colour (auto-detected per type from its first base sheet) to a common target — greens together for grass, whites for snow — keeping texture; dirt/rock/flowers untouched. ab_strength = hue+saturation pull, v_strength = mean-brightness leveling (0..1).
